tldr: Across 10 of 11 bucket groups, headlines frame this as either a territorial-sovereignty display (CULTURAL) or an impending FIFA sanction (CRIME_PUNISHMENT). The Daily Mail uniquely introduces a FAIRNESS angle — FIFA hypocrisy for banning an English fan's submarine flag while permitting Argentina's banner — while the Buenos Aires Herald is the only outlet to escalate to a security dimension, with its headline referencing a UK warship movement near the Malvinas within hours of the match. AfricaNews, alone among the 11 buckets, ran a headline covering only Argentina's semifinal qualification, omitting any reference to the banner or political controversy.

evidence.quote: Argentina protests UK warship movement near Malvinas

silences.what_they_covered_instead: AfricaNews' headline ('Argentina erupts after late win over England to reach World Cup final') covers only the sporting result and qualification for the final; the Falklands banner and political controversy do not appear in the title at all.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline to escalate beyond the banner incident to a military-diplomatic dimension, referencing a UK warship movement near the Malvinas — a second simultaneous flashpoint invisible in all other outlets' titles.

Only headline to frame the story as FIFA institutional hypocrisy, explicitly quoting 'hypocrisy' and contrasting the banned shipyard worker's submarine flag against Argentina's permitted Falklands banner — a FAIRNESS angle absent from every other outlet's title.

bottom_line: The headline layer reduces a multi-layered diplomatic incident to two dominant frames — Falklands/Malvinas sovereignty display and imminent FIFA sanction — stripping out the vice-presidential messaging, the UK warship movement, and the UK government's formal inquiry demand that characterised the body-text layer; only the Daily Mail's title introduces a fairness complaint, and only the Buenos Aires Herald's title reaches the security dimension.
